perturbation.original_text: 9.7 Governing Law and Jurisdiction This NDA shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Malaysia and the Parties hereto agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts in Malaysia.
perturbation.changed_text:
6.2.2 Any expenses including the legal costs arising from this clause and/or this NDA shall be borne by the defaulting party.

9.7 Governing Law and Jurisdiction This NDA shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction to be determined by the Receiving Party, and the Parties hereto agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts as selected by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The clause stating that the NDA is governed by Malaysian law and jurisdiction has been moved to a less prominent section and modified to give the Receiving Party the power to choose the governing law and jurisdiction. This could severely disadvantage the other party in case of disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 24 of the Contracts Act 1950 (Malaysia) implies that the consideration or object of an agreement is unlawful if it is of such a nature that, if permitted, it would defeat any law. By allowing one party to unilaterally select the governing law and jurisdiction, the agreement could be interpreted as enabling the selection of a jurisdiction that would undermine the legal protections otherwise available to the other party under Malaysian law, potentially defeating the purpose of the Contracts Act which expects fairness and balance in contractual relationships.

Definitions relating to Federal health care offense (a) As used in this title, the term "Federal health care offense" means a violation of, or a criminal conspiracy to violate- (1) section 669, 1035, 1347, or 1518 of this title or section 1128B of the Social Security Act ( 42 U.S.C. 1320a–7b ); or (2) section 287, 371, 664, 666, 1001, 1027, 1341, 1343, 1349, or 1954 of this title section 301 o
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original intent by allowing the Receiving Party to select the governing law and jurisdiction. This introduces a structural flaw by potentially enabling the selection of a jurisdiction that undermines legal protections available to the other party. The Law Explanation argues that Section 24 of the Contracts Act 1950 (Malaysia) implies that the consideration or object of an agreement is unlawful if it defeats any law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is irrelevant as it discusses 'Definitions relating to Federal health care offense', the core argument is that allowing one party to unilaterally choose the governing law creates a situation where fairness and balance in the contractual relationship, expected under Malaysian law, are compromised, constituting a legal contradiction as the original agreement was under Malaysian law and jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 The obligations of the Parties under this NDA shall continue and survive the completion of the Purpose and remain binding for a period of five ( 5) years from the date of completion of the Purpose or, from the date of the earlier termination of this NDA as provided in clause 7 herein.
perturbation.changed_text:
9.3 Assignment Neither Party shall be entitled to assign this NDA, in whole or in part, or any of its rights and obligations under this NDA without the prior written consent of the other Party.

5.2 The obligations of the Parties under this NDA shall continue and survive the completion of the Purpose and remain binding only until either party deems it necessary to disclose the confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The clause specifying the duration of confidentiality (5 years) is moved to a different part of the document and altered to state that it lasts only until a party wants to disclose. This makes the confidentiality clause essentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act 2010 (Malaysia) offers protection to trade secrets, but that protection relies on the secret being maintained for a specific duration agreed to in the contract. By making the confidentiality last only until either party wishes to disclose, the NDA undermines the very foundation upon which trade secret protection is built, rendering Section 6 of the Act ineffective regarding the agreement. There needs to be a reasonable period to provide some form of protection which this change removes.

perturbation.justification: The altered text introduces a significant contradiction. The original NDA specifies a 5-year confidentiality period. The changed version allows either party to disclose confidential information whenever they deem it 'necessary,' effectively nullifying the duration aspect of the agreement. The Law Explanation states that the change undermines the foundation of trade secret protection, as it removes any guaranteed period of confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: 2.  RIGHTS TO CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1 Use All Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party shall be used, consumed, utilised and acted in whatever ways by the Rec eiving Party solely and strictly for the Purpose, and each disclosure of Confidential Information may be recorded by the Personnel in Charge of each Party. The Receiving Party undertakes not to use the Confidential Information for further research, technic al analysis, reverse engineering and/or exploit for the purpose of any commercial gains in any manner whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text:
8. E-COMMUNICATION AND SIGNATURE The Parties hereby consent to electronic communication and electronic signatures being equal to signatures inked on paper. The Parties acknowledge and agree that electronic communication is an acceptable method of communicating information from a Party to the other party without having to communicate the same on paper. Any communication and subse quent electronic signature that has been sent or signed in the past, present, or future between the Parties will hold the same force and effect as a document signed and inked on paper.

2.1 Use All Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party shall be used in any manner the Rec eiving Party deems appropriate, and each disclosure of Confidential Information may be recorded by the Personnel in Charge of each Party.
perturbation.explanation: The restriction on the use of confidential information is removed. Now in section 8, the Receiving Party can use the information as they see fit, negating the entire point of a non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13 of the Copyright Act 1987 protects the exclusive rights of copyright holders. By allowing the Receiving Party to use Confidential Information in any manner, the modified clause permits potential copyright infringement if the Confidential Information includes copyrighted material. The Act aims to protect the rights of the copyright holder and this change effectively nullifies that protection by permitting unauthorised use.

perturbation.justification: The original text strictly limits the Receiving Party's use of Confidential Information to the Purpose of the agreement. The changed text removes this restriction, allowing the Receiving Party to use the Confidential Information 'in any manner the Receiving Party deems appropriate.' This directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is irrelevant, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both clearly outline how this change allows for potential misuse of copyrighted material, undermining copyright protections. By removing the limitation on use, the modified text creates a significant contradiction that could result in legal issues.

perturbation.original_text: 3. OBLIGATIONS 3.1 Receiving Party In relation to the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party undertakes - (a) to maintain the Confidential Information in strict confidence ; (b) to use the Confidential Information solely for the Purpose only and for no other purpose; (c) to refrain from copying, reproducing and reducing to writing or any other form of recording any part thereof except as may be reasonably necessary for the Purpos e provided that the Receiving Party has obtained the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; (d) to refrain from disclosing the Confidential Information whether to its employees (other than the Personnel in Charge) or third parties except to such of it s employees (other then the Personnel in Charge) or third parties who have a need to know or whose services are appropriately required in connection with the Purpose but subject to the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and the provision of clau se 3.2 herein below; (e) to apply no lesser security measures and degree of care thereto than those which the Receiving Party applies to its own Confidential Information or proprietary information and which the Receiving Party
perturbation.changed_text:
9.9.2 All annexures and appendices annexed hereto sh all form part of this NDA and shall be read taken and construed as an essential part of this NDA.

3. OBLIGATIONS 3.1 Receiving Party In relation to the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party undertakes absolutely no obligations.
perturbation.explanation: The Receiving Party's obligations to maintain confidentiality are completely removed and put in an unrelated section of the document about the appendices. This makes the core principle of the NDA unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 3 of the Trade Secrets Act 2010 (Malaysia) defines 'trade secret' and implies the existence of an obligation to maintain its secrecy. By stating that the Receiving Party undertakes absolutely no obligations, the contract actively contradicts this implied obligation. This makes the entire concept of a trade secret unenforceable under this agreement, as the Receiving Party has no duty to protect it, essentially negating the intention of the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text establishes clear obligations for the Receiving Party regarding confidential information, while the changed text explicitly states that the Receiving Party undertakes absolutely no obligations. This directly contradicts the implied obligation to maintain secrecy, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, rendering the protection of trade secrets unenforceable under the agreement.
